title: GADP project-init.json
description: Schema for the project-init.json config file consumed by gadp_init_project.py. Produces all nine initial GADP YAML files. Run: python3 gadp/scripts/gadp_init_project.py --config project-init.json

product.name: TaskFlow
product.tagline: Project management for small teams that actually ships

solution_map.problem: Small teams lose track of tasks across Slack, email, and sticky notes
solution_map.for_whom: Product and engineering teams of 2-15 people

capabilities.label: User can register with email and password
User can create and assign tasks

kpis.label: 100 active users within 90 days of launch

project_id.description: UUID v4 for this project. Generated automatically by gadp_init_project.py if absent. Provide it only when re-initialising an existing project to preserve the ID.

project_name.description: Human-readable project name. Used as the primary identifier in GADP files and the audit log.

product_type.description: Product category. Determines which GADP invariants, quality intent defaults, and CI/CD templates are activated. 'Composite' is for products that span multiple categories.

has_ui.description: True if the product has any user-facing interface (web, desktop, extension popup). When true: design-language.yaml is generated, UI contracts are expected, INV-DQ-* design token invariants are active, and the Builder reads frontend skills for UI contracts.

has_backend.description: True if the product has a server-side application layer. When true: openapi.yaml stub is generated, API contracts are expected, and the invariant system includes auth and error response shape checks.

has_database.description: True if the product uses a persistent database. When true: ORM-only invariant (INV-D-001) is active, migration requirements apply to schema contracts, and the data lifecycle section of the intent store is populated.

has_auth.description: True if the product has user authentication. When true: auth-specific invariants are active (httpOnly cookie, RBAC server-side), auth contracts are expected in Sprint 1, and STRIDE threat model includes authentication threats.

regulatory_exposure.description: Regulatory framework the product operates under. Determines which mandatory quality intents and compliance contracts are added. HIPAA takes precedence over GDPR if both apply — model two separate projects or use HIPAA only. PCI-DSS uses scope reduction logic: cardholder data never touches application servers.

data_sensitivity.description: Sensitivity classification of the data the product handles. SENSITIVE: PII, health data, financial records. RESTRICTED: credentials, cryptographic material, regulated health information. Affects which threat mitigations are mandatory.

product.description: Product identity block. Written to intent-store.yaml → product. If absent, gadp_init_project.py generates a stub — replace it before running the Intent Architect.

name.description: Product name as it will appear to users and in marketing material.

tagline.description: One sentence value proposition. Should name the problem, the audience, and the differentiator.

solution_map.description: Problem-solution framing. Used by the Intent Architect to derive capability intents and by the Outcome Resolver to prioritise architectural decisions.

problem.description: The specific problem the product solves. One to three sentences.

for_whom.description: Who experiences the problem — be specific. 'Small teams' is better than 'people'.

severity.description: How severe the problem is for the target audience. Affects Sprint 1 scope prioritisation.

stack_preferences.description: Preferred technology stack. All fields are optional — the Outcome Resolver selects defaults based on product_type and has_* flags if absent. Preferences here are treated as strong signals, not hard requirements.

orm.description: ORM or query builder. Example: 'Prisma', 'SQLAlchemy', 'ActiveRecord'. Required if has_database is true — INV-D-001 (ORM only, no raw SQL) is active.

auth_strategy.description: Authentication approach. Example: 'Custom JWT', 'NextAuth.js', 'Clerk'. If has_auth is true and this is absent, the Outcome Resolver selects a default appropriate to the framework.

capabilities.description: Capability intents — what the product can do. At least one core capability is required if this array is non-empty. If empty or absent, the Intent Architect generates these interactively from the product description.

id.description: Unique capability intent ID in CI-NNN format. Must be unique across the full capabilities list.

label.description: Capability statement written from the user's perspective. 'User can X' or 'System can X'. Be specific enough that a test can verify it.

scope.description: Sprint assignment scope. core: Sprint 1 candidates. extension: Sprint 2+. future: explicitly deferred — requires inclusion_trigger.

security_surface.description: True if this capability handles credentials, session tokens, PII, or any data that would generate a STRIDE threat. Triggers automatic STRIDE threat derivation for this capability.

security_concern_type.description: Type of security concern. Required when security_surface is true. Determines which STRIDE threat categories are applied.

inferred.description: True if this capability was derived by the Intent Architect from other capabilities, not stated explicitly by the user. Examples: password reset inferred from auth, email verification inferred from registration.

deferral_reason.description: Why this capability is deferred. Required when scope is 'future'.

inclusion_trigger.description: The condition that would move this capability into scope. Required when scope is 'future'. Example: 'When MRR exceeds $10k/month' or 'When enterprise tier is launched'.

quality_intents.description: Quality targets that apply to the whole product, not a single feature. Mandatory quality intents (LCP, CLS, INP, bundle size, a11y, uptime, error rate) are added automatically by gadp_init_project.py based on product_type — do not duplicate them here.

id.description: Unique quality intent ID. QI-LCP, QI-CLS, QI-INP, QI-BUNDLE, QI-A11Y, QI-UPTIME, QI-ERROR-RATE are reserved for mandatory intents.

label.description: Quality target in measurable terms. Include the metric, the threshold, and the condition. Example: 'LCP <= 2.5s at p75 on median mobile hardware'.

type.description: hard: sprint gate fails if this target is not met. soft: tracked, surfaced as an audit flag, does not block the gate.

scale_trigger.description: The condition under which this quality target must be re-evaluated. Example: 'p75 LCP exceeds 2.5s for three consecutive deploys'.

measurement_method.description: How this quality target is measured. Example: 'Lighthouse CI', 'Datadog SLO', 'k6 load test'.

security_intents.description: Explicit security requirements beyond what STRIDE derives automatically. Most security requirements are generated by the Outcome Resolver — use this field only for requirements that come from a security review or compliance mandate not covered by STRIDE.

id.description: Unique security intent ID in SI-NNN format.

source.description: Where this requirement comes from. Example: 'PCI-DSS 4.0 requirement 6.4', 'Internal security review 2025-03'.

constraints.description: Hard constraints that apply to the project — budget, timeline, team size, technology mandates. These are not quality targets — they are fixed parameters that the Outcome Resolver must work within.

id.description: Unique constraint ID in CON-NNN format.

label.description: Constraint stated precisely. Example: 'Must deploy to Vercel — no custom server infrastructure'.

kpis.description: Business KPIs that define success for this product. At most one should have north_star: true.

id.description: Unique KPI ID in KPI-NNN format.

label.description: KPI stated with a specific target and timeframe. Example: '100 active users within 90 days of launch'.

north_star.description: True for the single most important KPI. At most one KPI should be the north star.

assumptions.description: Assumptions the product is built on that have not been validated. Surfaced in the intent store as risks. Example: 'Users will prefer email registration over OAuth'.

id.description: Unique assumption ID in ASM-NNN format.

label.description: The assumption stated as a falsifiable claim.

validation_method.description: How this assumption will be validated. Example: 'User interviews with 10 target users before Sprint 2'.

contracts.description: Pre-populated contracts. Normally empty at init — the Outcome Resolver generates contracts after architecture decisions are made. Include contracts here only when bootstrapping from an existing project-init.json that already has defined contracts.

items.description: Contract stub. See gadp_append_contract.py for the full contract schema and validation rules.

design.description: Design system configuration. Only required when has_ui is true and design tokens are known before the Intent Architect runs. If absent and has_ui is true, gadp_init_project.py generates a design-language.yaml stub with placeholder tokens.

source.description: How the design language was derived. 'described': tokens derived from text description during the Intent Architect session. 'figma': tokens extracted from a Figma file. 'uploaded': tokens from an uploaded style guide.

tokens.description: Design token values to pre-populate in design-language.yaml.

component_library.description: UI component library. Example: 'shadcn/ui', 'Radix UI', 'Chakra UI', 'Headless UI', 'None'.

css_approach.description: CSS methodology. Example: 'Tailwind CSS', 'CSS Modules', 'Styled Components'.

colors.description: Color token values. These map to Tailwind CSS theme tokens — use semantic names, not raw hex values. The INV-DQ-001 invariant enforces that no hex values appear in source files; these values configure the theme, they do not appear in component code.
